text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing adhesions from previous abdominal surgeries within the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the first portion of the duodenum and performs a retroduodenal dissection, creating a window and fully separating the duodenum from surrounding tissues.
ICG fluorescence imaging is utilized to assess the perfusion of the duodenum following its dissection, highlighting the vascular supply to the tissue.
The surgeon transects the duodenum using a surgical stapler equipped with a tan load, visibly separating the duodenal tissue.
Following the transection of the duodenum, the surgeon assesses the perfusion of both the distal and proximal duodenum using ICG fluorescence, with the gastroduodenal artery clearly visible.
The surgeon brings the marked jejunal loop, located 250 centimeters from the ileocecal valve, up in a supracolic fashion to prepare for the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side anastomosis with a posterior running layer using III-O PDS suture, connecting the jejunal loop to the duodenum.
The surgeon transects the jejunum three centimeters from the anastomosis using a surgical stapler, completing the separation of the intestinal segment.
The surgeon performs a two-centimeter duodenotomy and enterotomy using a monopolar hook, creating openings in the duodenum and adjacent intestinal segment.
The surgeon performs an inner posterior running suture layer using 3-0 PDS suture material, reinforcing the anastomosis site.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis by applying two anterior running layers, both inner and outer, using 3-0 PDS sutures.
The surgeon creates two small enterotomies using a monopolar hook, one at the biliary limb and another at the second jejunal marking, located 130 centimeters from the ileocecal valve.
The surgeon performs an enteroenterostomy using a stapler, followed by closure of the enterostomies in two uninterrupted layers with 3-0 PDS sutures.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to inspect the surgical site, confirming there is no visible evidence of a leak.
